nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rocky and gravelly rivers and streams in hill and foothills zones; also found in slower tributaries and floodplain channels during monsoon season. Prefers areas with abundant cover — submerged roots, leaf litter, and boulders.

decorPreferences: smooth rounded river pebbles and gravel substrate
driftwood caves and tangles for shelter
robust rooted plants (Java fern, Anubias, Microsorum) anchored to hardscape
dim areas or overhangs to replicate shaded river margins

diet.notes: Strongly snail-eating and insect-leaning omnivore. Excellent natural controller of pest snails (ramshorns, bladder snails) — often recommended alongside clown loaches for this purpose. Core diet: high-quality sinking pellets and wafers (Hikari Sinking Wafers, NLS Bottom Feeder), frozen bloodworms, frozen tubifex, frozen daphnia, and live or frozen brine shrimp. Blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach) accepted. Snails of all sizes relished. Supplement with algae wafers to round out plant matter intake. Feed after lights-out to match nocturnal foraging tendencies.

goodWith: other Botiidae loaches (kept in separate species groups)
avoidWith: dwarf shrimp (neocaridina, caridina) — will be eaten
very small fish under 3 cm (fry risk)
snails intended to be kept (consumed readily)
small peaceful bottom-dwellers that compete for hiding spots

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo record for Botia dario. Check FishBase species page for current image availability and license details.

summary: The Bengal Loach (Botia dario) is a boldly striped, social loach from northeastern India and Bangladesh reaching 12–15 cm. A mandatory shoaling species (5+ individuals), it is a voracious snail eater and an active, personable addition to mid-to-large community tanks with good flow.
careNotes:
The Bengal Loach is native to the Brahmaputra and Ganges river drainages of northeastern India (West Bengal, Assam) and Bangladesh, where it inhabits fast-flowing, oxygen-rich rivers and hill streams. It is taxonomically placed in the family Botiidae — formerly treated as a subfamily of Cobitidae, now widely recognised as a distinct family following molecular phylogenetic work. It is closely related to and frequently confused with the Zebra Loach (Botia striata) and the Comb-tail Loach (Botia histrionica); B. dario can be distinguished by its more numerous and irregular body stripes extending well onto the fins.

A minimum group of five is non-negotiable: solitary or pair-kept specimens become stressed, hide permanently, and often display chronic disease susceptibility. Larger groups (6–10+) produce richer social behaviour including play-chasing, mock sparring, and synchronised foraging. Intragroup squabbling is normal and not harmful as long as the tank is large enough (120 cm footprint minimum) and ample hiding places are provided. Interspecific aggression is low towards robust mid-water fish.

High dissolved oxygen and moderate water movement should be provided — an additional powerhead or spray bar is recommended. Temperature range 22–27 °C; values below 20 °C or above 28 °C are stressful. Soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.5–7.5, gH 5–15) suits them well. Sensitive to deteriorating water quality; perform regular 25–30% weekly water changes.

Botia dario possesses a sub-orbital spine (bifurcated lachrymal bone) that can erect when the fish is stressed — use fine mesh nets to avoid entanglement, and handle only when necessary. Clicking sounds produced by the loach’s pharyngeal teeth are normal social communication and a sign of a settled group.

For pest snail control, Bengal Loaches are highly effective; they will systematically eliminate ramshorn, bladder, and pond snails from a tank. They should not be housed with any snails intended to be kept as display animals.
breedingNotes: Breeding Botia dario in captivity is rarely achieved and has not been reliably documented under standard aquarium conditions. In their native range, spawning is believed to be triggered by monsoon flooding — a dramatic drop in water temperature, increased flow, and reduced hardness associated with seasonal rainfall. Hormone-induced spawning has been used in commercial fish farms in India and Bangladesh for aquaculture supply, but this is not accessible to hobbyists. Attempting to simulate monsoon conditions (cool water change, elevated flow, reduction in hardness) may stimulate spawning behaviour in a well-conditioned group, but verified home-aquarium successes are extremely rare. Breedability is therefore classified as effectively not achievable in standard captivity for most hobbyists.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Botia dario
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (India/Bangladesh, Brahmaputra/Ganges drainages), adult size (to 15 cm), temperature (22–26°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness, substrate preferences, social requirements (groups of 5+), diet, and taxonomy. Botiidae family placement and distinction from B. striata and B. histrionica confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Botia dario
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (reported to 15 cm TL), native range (Ganges and Brahmaputra drainages, NE India and Bangladesh), taxonomy (Botiidae, Cypriniformes), temperature range, and diet (invertebrates, detritus, plant matter). Confirmed classification within Botiidae.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: General loach husbandry guidance used for shoaling requirements, snail-eating behaviour, tank setup (caves, driftwood), and feeding recommendations (sinking foods, snails as supplement). Confirmed group-size minimum of 5+ and semi-aggressive temperament within species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Botia dario profile
additional.notes: Species profile used for confirmation of common name (Bengal Loach / Queen Loach), natural habitat description (fast-flowing rocky rivers, NE India), social behaviour notes, and snail predation. Confirmed intermediate care level and minimum tank size recommendations.

additional.name: Loaches Online — Botia dario species profile
additional.notes: Specialist loach resource. Used for sub-orbital spine care note, clicking behaviour as social communication, confusion with B. striata and B. histrionica, intragroup social dynamics, and monsoon-breeding hypothesis. Confirmed breeding as extremely rare in captivity without hormones.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable home-aquarium breeding reported. Commercial production uses hormone injection to mimic monsoon spawning triggers. Attempting monsoon simulation (cool water change, increased flow, reduced hardness) may induce spawning behaviour but verified fry production by hobbyists is extremely rare.
